1.00

"Rational" Current Market Cap

$10.17B

The following are my best estimates of different likely scenarios as to the future value of the company:

  • Base: sub-q wins; oral is acceptable (maintenance); MASH progresses → $12-15B fair value.
  • Upside: sub-q wins; oral is better than expected (adopted as a standalone initial agent for broader use, not just post-VK2735 sub-q maintenance—or, alternatively, as a maintenance agent for patients who achieved weight loss through non-VKTX agents); MASH positive → $25–$50B.
  • Downside: the company struggles as sub-q disappoints; oral is not established even for maintenance; MASH gains no serious traction; market cap sinks to well below $1B.

To the bears who argue that Lilly and Novo are miles ahead and that winner-take-all dynamics will prevail, I respectfully disagree. The essential question is not whether Viking outruns them, but whether there is room for a strong #3—even setting aside the dream scenario in which VK2735 knocks both Lilly and Novo from their leadership positions.

Ask Dr Pepper how that works in beverages: a durable third brand can still be immensely valuable if the category is large enough—and if you acquired the Dr Pepper of the weight-loss market at a fraction of its fair value before it left the gate. The markets for obesity drugs (and, coincidentally, sugar-loaded soda that has inflated the waistlines of our species) are both big and durable.

The Buyout Game: Big Pharma Or Private Equity

I speculate that Brian Lian, a scientist and former Wall Street insider, has probably driven a hard bargain with any potential suitors from the Big Pharma universe as he prepares the company to obtain FDA approvals and to market and deliver VK2735 on its own. The most powerful bargaining chip he has, after the clinical data of VK2735, is probably the deal with CordenPharma, as it represents the company's alternative to any inferior offer on the table.

I also wish to raise the possibility of a PE buyout. A $3 billion market value is within the reach of multi-fund PE complexes. Several funds together (or a single heavyweight) can pay more than Big Pharma, take Viking private, finish Phase 3 on both forms, and exit to public markets at a much higher implied value or keep the company as the cash cow.

Why would PE do it? The returns pencil if sub-q is approved—even without oral (which I still expect to survive). Viking is clean: cash, no debt, locked-in with industrial-scale production commitment. Dr. Brian Lian may prefer control and timing—PE can offer that in ways strategic buyers often do not.

The lack of any buyout may disappoint those who bought VKTX for a quick reward. However, if management, led by Dr. Lian, sees the entire clinical program and commercial launches through to fully realize the pipeline’s value, long-term investors will be handsomely rewarded.

The Cash Runway

At mid-2025, Viking reported approximately $808 million in cash and equivalents and a market capitalization near $2.8 billion. R&D expenses were rising into the $60 million-per-quarter range, consistent with launching two Phase III trials, while net operating cash outflow for the first half of 2025 was about $99 million. Even assuming a 30 percent increase in burn, Viking has a runway of four years or more — ample time to reach key clinical trial and regulatory approval milestones.

That war chest also creates optionality: Viking can partner selectively, negotiate from strength, or choose to go it alone through pivotal readouts.

Risks to My Thesis

At the risk of stating the obvious, investing in clinical-stage biotech companies is not for the faint of heart, or the impatient traders or smart chart readers (no offense), or for anyone who does not have the requisite patience to understand and analyze the clinical data. The following risks stand out:

  • Clinical Trials. Phase 3 clinical trials are difficult. Sub-q could miss a primary endpoint or reveal an unforeseen safety signal. Oral could remain “good but finicky” on tolerability despite titration discipline. VK2809’s further clinical trial design could slip as endpoints evolve.
  • Competitive. Novo’s oral semaglutide and Lilly’s orforglipron will continue to be formidable in the foreseeable future; newer tri-agonists are emerging. The crowding effect is real and can compress Viking’s addressable slice.
  • Operation and Execution. In addition to marketing and distribution, scaling to physically produce >1B tablets and/or ~100M devices annually is nothing trivial, even with a reputable contract manufacturer. The tech transfer, QA/QC, and other supply chain challenges all involve substantial execution risks for VKTX. Such risks have been somewhat mitigated, but not eliminated, by the CordenPharma deal structure.
  • Financing. While current cash funds the pivotal Phase III clinical trials, more financing will be required for commercial operations.
  • Psychology. If large drawdowns (see: –40% day) make you feel nauseated, you should not buy shares of VKTX—or, for that matter, even consider enrolling in a VK2735 clinical trial.

Conclusion

Viking stock appears to be a mispriced asymmetric bet with the odds favoring bulls. The probability that all three programs fail is not zero, but it is nowhere near Mr. Market’s implied odds. The aggregate of the probability-weighted value of the injectable VK2735, oral VK2735, and VK2809 far exceeds today’s market cap of VKTX.

Based upon the foregoing, I assign a speculative BUY to VKTX with a price target range between $100 and $200, depending on the clinical trial results, market sentiments, and other things. Any long-term investor of VKTX, however, does need the right temperament to be able to watch your position drop 40% of its value in a day, and still review the clinical data (and your brokerage statement) calmly without losing sight of both the big picture and the critical nuances.

On a final note, I plan to revisit this stock periodically as more clinical trial data become available.

data.title: Fair Issac Vs. Equifax: The Credit Score War Has Begun
data.description:
The Credit Score Industry Is Changing

"Your Fair Isaac Corporation (NYSE:FICO)("Fico") article didn't age well". This is how some commented on my recent piece of research, where I revised my rate of Fico from buy to sell. I wrote it a little more than a week ago, signaling that the new regulatory environment would have led to an inevitable monopoly erosion with the introduction of the VantageScore 4.0 for mortgage underwriting. Secondly, I pointed out that a loss of pricing power, even though we would have still been in an oligopolistic environment, would have been likely undermined for some time. Third, I pointed out the issue of Fico competing now directly with the three credit bureaus that are data suppliers to Fico and that could now compete with it without paying for data access. Finally, I also showed that Fico was increasing its lobbying expenses while its prior buybacks had increased its leverage, limiting further flexibility on share repurchases if the weakened growth outlook turned into reality.

At the same time, I analyzed Equifax (NYSE:EFX), saying that it could be a nice bet as it would have "scavenged" on Fico's regulatory woes. Nonetheless, I rated it as a hold because I saw it as too expensive.

You probably know that I am writing a new update on the two. Something very big happened that could truly change the situation. In short, Fico launched a direct license program for mortgage lending. Fico soared 18% the day after the announcement, while Equifax lost almost 8%, with the other two credit bureaus (TransUnion and Experian) trading down too.

Yes, Wall Street and many others - including myself - were surprised by this bold move.

In short, FICO had been under regulatory scrutiny as the FHFA Director Bill Pulte blamed the company for having flexed its pricing power too boldly over the last few years. On the day the announcement was public, Mr. Pulte posted on X that Fico's decision "is a first step". He then addressed the credit bureaus.

I encourage the Credit Bureau’s to also take similar creative and constructive actions to make our markets safer, stronger, and more competitive. To that end, “Vantage Score” should also look at ensuring they are competitive, in every way, including but not limited to costs. Thank you to all who are helping make the Market more competitive, and safer and sounder, for the benefit of the American People!

Let's see what happened and why this announcement is a game-changer.

First of all, we need to understand the roles both companies played in the industry. Equifax is the largest U.S. credit bureau. This means that it collects and puts together data on credit by combining credit card movements, loan repayment history, and similar information to create one credit report per customer. In other words, Equifax collects raw data. Fico, on the other hand, doesn't do this but buys from Equifax and the other credit bureaus their credit reports, but it owns the scoring models and the algorithms needed to create them. Once a Fico score comes out, Fico sells it to its distributors (mainly the three credit bureaus). So, Fico had a monopoly on the scoring model, but relied on the data from the credit bureaus. These three created the VantageScore 4.0, which is now accepted by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac.

The introduction of the VS 4.0 means Fico has a competitor. This put Fico in a rough spot. In fact, its business model worked in this way. The company collected data from the credit bureaus that it then used to generate its FICO score, which was sent back to the bureaus. These are distributed the score to credit reporting agencies, mortgage lenders, and similar.

This was true until the recent announcement. Now, FICO has decided to change the way the FICO score is delivered to the mortgage industry. Tri-merge resellers can now have direct access and distribute the FICO score, which means they won't pay the markup charged by credit bureaus. Fico will charge $4.95 per score, with an additional $33 per score only if the mortgage is approved. The other option is to pay $10 per score. According to Fico, this means that score fees will be reduced by 50%. Of course, credit bureaus won't be part of the supply chain, and this will result in almost intact pricing for Fico and savings for the end customer.

What this means for the two companies

Fico

Fico has somewhat disentangled itself from a lethal embrace with the credit bureaus. It now no longer depends on them to distribute its scores, defending therefore its proprietary scoring model (which is clearly the industry standard), as well as its profits. Moreover, it has launched a pricing model that links part of its revenues to closed loans. Since we are expecting refinancing to go up through 2026 as rates go down, Fico is going to benefit from positive momentum.

While Fico gains control of distribution, it still has to face competition when it comes to scoring models. This means that it will have to keep prices flat for some time because the VS 4.0 will surely try to undercut it in price.

Let's do some back-of-the-envelope calculations.

According to the September 2025 Economic and Housing Outlook released by Fannie Mae, single-family mortgage originations should $2.32T in 2026 (previous forecast $2.26T), with an average loan size of around 380k. This means that, if we divide the two, we get that we will have around 6.1M loans next year. Considering that each loan still requires 3 scores and that Fico owns the market, we can expect that for each mortgage, Fico earns 3*($4.95+$33). This is equal to $113,85. Let's say that next year, Fico will have 85% of the market share and will thus sell its score for 5.19M loans. The result would be $590M in revenue.

Now Fico reports its results, splitting them into two segments: Scores and Software. However, we don't know the breakout by score type (mortgage, auto, credit card). Last year, Fico reported $920M in Scores. Assuming that mortgages could be around 55%, we could guess that mortgage scores reached $506M in revenue. The result I estimate for 2026 is good, but it is also mainly driven by an increase in the number of loans. Clearly, if Fico loses more market share and it goes below 70%, we would have a projected revenue below $486, which would not help the company's top line.

Equifax

First of all, Equifax has its best business in Workforce Solutions, with The Work Number that sells income and employment verification services to companies. In particular, this segment continues to win and increase its volumes through agreements and contracts with the government, both at the federal and state levels. In fact, there are about 100M people in the U.S. who are getting some form of social services. To qualify, they have to prove that they are in need. This is basically an income verification. Equifax estimates that every year $160B is incorrectly paid because income verification procedures are still mainly manual. So, this is the great tailwind for the company that we should keep in mind as we assess its prospects.

The segment that is linked to mortgages is U.S. Information Solutions (USIS). Surely, USIS economics are now pressured because Fico will sell directly to resellers, bypassing Equifax. At the same time, Equifax has the raw data and still earns a fee every time credit reports are pulled. Moreover, it can push its VS 4.0 to steal market share from Fico. Since we expect loans to increase through 2026, Equifax is set to benefit from the same tailwind as Fico. As we have seen, there is a headline risk because of markup loss on distributing scores. On the other hand, Equifax can leverage its distribution network to now sell its VS 4.0. However, its core business of collecting raw data and selling the Work number should benefit from a more general recovery in the loan market.

Bottom line: who wins

Well, the short-term winner is surely Fico. It has managed to announce an unexpected move, which has renewed enthusiasm among investors. Medium-term risk is still there, however, since it is called market share erosion as the VS 4.0 penetrates the market.

The outlook for Equifax remains mixed. The likelihood of seeing a part of USIS compress due to the direct-to-lender strategy announced by Fico is high. But it could be compensated by higher VS 4.0 score sales, as the company is now more incentivized to grow its market share in credit scores.

Let's say Equifax loses its $2-$3 markup on each score, it could lose around $40-50M per year. This is not an extremely big hit considering that the company reported total yearly revenues were $5.7B, with $2.3B coming from WS and $1.9B from USIS. I would look at WS to understand where Equifax's true strength lies. At the same time, right now, Equifax loses to Fico's resale strategy the earnings coming from markups. Since its valuation is steep and prices in some growth, we should be cautious.

In the near term, I estimate that Fico's move could add around 2 percentage points to its FCF CAGR baseline, which moves from 8% to 10%. This means that it could generate $900M in FCF next year. In a DCF with an 8% discount rate, we have that its fair price should be around $1,500. Right now, the stock is back at $1,850, and it is clearly benefiting from strong momentum. We are talking about a fwd PE of 68 and a fwd EV/EBITDA of 41. While I would not short the stock now, I would consider taking some nice gains. The mid-term outlook suggests that market share erosion will take place, and this could dampen Fico's growth rates, leading to multiple compression.

When we do the same calculations on Equifax, due to pressure on USIS, we could cut its FCF growth rate from 8.5% to 6.5%. This means that next year the company could report $950M in FCF. Using the same discount rate, we have a fair value of $190. Right now, the stock is around $235, and I still see that it is $50 too expensive.

As strange as this might seem, although investors cheered (and rightly so, as I have explained), the announcement brought Fico's stock back into overvalued territory, with a deteriorating outlook that could soon become reality. I don't expect Fico to go belly up; after all, it will still be part of an important duopoly. But I expect its growth to slow down a bit. Therefore, I rate Fico once again a sell. I like the setup for Equifax mid- to long-term. But I also think its valuation right now is steep. Its fwd PE is 40, and its fwd EV/EBITDA is 17.3. While these multiples are a bit better than Fico's, they still don't give room to a reasonable margin of safety. So, I rate Equifax as a hold.

What should we do? I think we will need two more quarters to see how Fico will compete with Equifax and the other two credit bureaus. This will help us feel the pulse of the changing market. But, for now, though I think Fico's move was brilliant, I don't think it will be able to offset the inevitable market erosion it has to suffer because of the regulator's will.

data.title: Lennox: Attractive Valuations And Impending Recovery In 2026 Make It A Good Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last covered Lennox International (NYSE:LII) in April with a Buy rating. The stock did well into midsummer but has since pulled back. That correction has mostly been tied to tough industry conditions - namely the drag from R410A inventory destocking and the canister shortage tied to the new R454B refrigerant. Those factors pressured sales and weighed on sentiment across residential HVAC names.

I see those as temporary headwinds. Looking out to 2026 and beyond, the setup looks much better. Sales should benefit from an end-market recovery as the rate cycle turns more supportive. The Fed has already started cutting, which over time should ease mortgage pressure and help both residential and light commercial demand.

On top of the macro, Lennox is executing well. The company has strengthened its distribution footprint, improved service levels, and is now leaning into emergency replacement demand thanks to added capacity at its New Mexico facility. The balance sheet is in good shape, with net leverage at just 1.0x EBITDA, leaving room for bolt-on M&A to complement organic growth.

Margins are another bright spot. Despite volume pressure, Lennox has been able to expand profitability through pricing, mix, and cost discipline. As volumes recover, operating leverage should provide another tailwind, alongside ongoing productivity and supply chain initiatives.

All in, I expect growth to re-accelerate in 2026 and beyond. With the stock trading below its historical P/E multiple, I think there's room for a re-rate as fundamentals improve. Between cyclical recovery, solid execution, and balance sheet flexibility, the risk/reward looks attractive. I'm reiterating my Buy rating.

Revenue Analysis And Outlook

Lennox reported about 3% revenue growth last quarter, with sales coming in at $1.50B. The top line was driven almost entirely by pricing and mix, which added roughly 11% and more than offset an 8% volume decline.

By segment, Home Comfort Solutions revenue was up 3% year-over-year. Volumes were down about 9%, pressured by both soft end-market demand and the drag from R410A destocking, with some added impact from R454B canister shortages. Price and mix were strong, though, up 12%, which more than carried the segment.

Building Climate Solutions grew nearly 5%. Volumes dipped 3% given broader market weakness, but emergency replacement activity provided a partial offset, and pricing/mix was again positive at 8%.

Overall, the quarter reflected resilient pricing power and strong execution, even as volume trends remained tough.

Moving forward, in the near term, Lennox is still dealing with some volume headwinds across its core residential and light commercial markets. While rates have started reversing, we are in a slow new construction season, and it will take a couple of quarters till next spring season for the sales to see a good pickup. On top of that, the refrigerant transition is creating a bit of noise. Distributors are focused on clearing out R410A systems before the year-end deadline, which means less pull-through on the new R454B units. That destocking dynamic probably sticks around through the rest of 2025.

Once we get past this transition, though, the setup looks much cleaner. Starting in 2026, the channel should be free of R410A inventory, which should help factory shipments and sell-through line up better. By then, we'll also be further along in the interest rate cycle, so both new and existing home sales should start to pick back up. The housing backdrop is still fundamentally solid, with a lot of pent-up demand sitting on the sidelines, and I'd expect the next cooling season to show a healthier demand.

On top of the macro, Lennox has some company-specific drivers that should help. The new factory at Saltillo, Mexico, has given it the capacity to go after the emergency replacement market in a way it really couldn't before. With better fulfillment and next-day delivery, the sales team is leaning into that channel, and it feels like a solid three- to five-year growth leg. Distribution is another piece. Between recent bolt-ons like the HVAC division of NSI's DuroDyne, and Supco, and ongoing investments to boost service levels, Lennox is strengthening its contractor reach and creating a real advantage versus peers. And with net leverage sitting at just 1.0x EBITDA, the balance sheet gives them room for more bolt-on M&A while still investing in the core.

The longer-term story is also tied to energy efficiency. Power costs keep moving up, data centers are adding to grid demand, and regulations keep pushing toward lower emissions. All of that plays into more efficient HVAC systems. We have recently seen a transition to R454B refrigerant from R410a, which helped the company's price/mix, and I am expecting more such transitions moving forward.

Put it all together, and while the next couple of quarters might still feel bumpy, the path into 2026 looks like a clean re-acceleration story.

Margin Analysis And Outlook

Margins came in strong in Q2, with Lennox posting a 170 bps year-over-year improvement and segment profit margin reaching 23.6%. The lift was driven by positive price/mix and solid execution on cost controls.

Home Comfort Solutions was the standout, with return on sales expanding 200 bps to 25.3%. That reflected strong pricing gains alongside good operational discipline. Building Climate Solutions also moved higher, up 60 bps year-over-year to 24.9%, supported by pricing and factory productivity improvements that offset inflationary headwinds.

Overall, the quarter showed that even with volumes under pressure, Lennox is managing to protect and even expand profitability through a combination of pricing power and efficiency.

I'm optimistic about Lennox's margin setup over the medium term. As volumes start to recover with the housing cycle and the R410A inventory clears, the company should get solid operating leverage. Higher throughput tends to flow through at pretty healthy increments, and with rate cuts starting to take hold, demand in 2026 looks like a good setup.

On costs, management has been running a tight playbook. They're targeting about $75 million in annual savings through their operational excellence program, and we're already seeing steady factory productivity gains. Lean manufacturing and Six Sigma are now embedded across the footprint, with projects around capacity optimization and automation helping push throughput higher while pulling labor cost per unit lower. Utilization at the Mexico facility should keep improving too, which adds another layer of margin support.

Tariffs and material inflation are still in the mix near term, but Lennox is working on the offsets. Strategic sourcing, supplier switches, and contract resets are all helping, and the company has enough pricing power and favorable mix from new R454B and higher-efficiency products to cover the rest.

Put it all together, margins should keep trending higher into 2026 and beyond. Volume recovery brings leverage, efficiency programs add structure, and mix shifts toward premium products provide incremental lift.

Valuation And Rating

Lennox is trading at 23.18x FY25 consensus EPS of $23.58 and 21.11x FY26 consensus EPS of $25.89. Over the past five years, the stock has averaged about 23.5x forward earnings. Since we are already approaching the end of this fiscal year, I believe it makes sense to look at next year's numbers, and on FY26 earnings, the stock is available at an attractive discount.

I think there's good potential for the growth to pick up and valuation multiple to re-rate as temporary headwinds roll off. Issues like refrigerant canister shortages, R410A destocking, and the drag from high mortgage rates are set to fade, and the rate cycle should turn more supportive into 2026. In addition, the company is executing well, as is evident from the margin improvement we saw last quarter.

If the stock were to revert to its five-year average multiple of 23.5x on FY26 consensus EPS of $25.89, it would imply a fair value of about $608 per share. That represents over 10% upside from the current price of ~$550. In addition, investors also get a ~1% dividend yield.

All in, with end markets improving, margins trending higher, and a solid balance sheet that supports both reinvestment and bolt-on M&A, I see the risk/reward as favorable. I'm maintaining a Buy rating on the stock.

Risks

I'm expecting lower rates to help unlock demand, especially in housing and replacement markets, but if the Fed cuts more slowly than expected or mortgage rates stay elevated longer, the recovery could be weaker than I'm building in. That would mean softer volumes and a slower revenue rebound, which would weigh on the stock.

Another risk is around M&A. Lennox has a good track record with bolt-on deals, but inorganic growth always carries extra uncertainty compared to organic execution. There's the possibility of overpaying for an asset, failing to realize synergies, or running into integration issues. Any of those could dilute returns and weigh on sentiment.
